Value Memory Layouts (MonoMove Runtime)
========================================

Primitive (e.g. u64)
--------------------

Frame slot
┌────────┐
│ value  │  8 bytes
└────────┘


Inline struct (e.g. { a: u64, b: u64 })
----------------------------------------

Frame slot
┌────────┬────────┐
│   a    │   b    │  16 bytes, flat
└────────┴────────┘


Heap struct
-----------

Frame slot                         Heap
┌────────┐                  ┌──────────────────────────┐
│   ●────┼─────────────────►│ desc_id(4) | size(4)     │  header
│        │                  ├──────────────────────────┤
└────────┘                  │ field_0                   │
  8 bytes                   │ field_1                   │
                            │ ...                       │
                            └──────────────────────────┘


Vector
------

Frame slot (vec)    Fat pointer (vec_ref)          Heap (data buffer)
┌────────┐          ┌────────┐              ┌──────────────────────────┐
│ ●──────┼─ ─ ─ ─ ─│ base ●─┼─ ─ ─ ┐      │ desc_id(4) | size(4)     │  header
└────────┘          │ offset │      ▼      ├──────────────────────────┤
  8 bytes           └────────┘   *(base     │ length (u64)             │
  (or null            16 bytes    +offset)  ├──────────────────────────┤
  if empty)                     = &vec_ptr  │ elem_0                   │
                                            │ elem_1                   │
  Vec instructions operate through the      │ ...                       │
  fat pointer (vec_ref), not the owned      └──────────────────────────┘
  slot directly. VecPushBack writes
  updated pointers back through the ref.    Capacity is derived from the header:
                                              cap = (size - 16) / elem_size
  A null vec_ptr means an empty vector
  with no heap allocation.


Heap struct containing a vector field
-------------------------------------

Frame slot       Fat pointer (vec_ref)     Heap (struct)                  Heap (data buffer)
┌────────┐       ┌────────┐          ┌──────────────────────┐      ┌──────────────────────┐
│   ●────┼──────►│ base ●─┼─────────►│ desc_id(4) | size(4) │      │ desc_id(4) | size(4) │
└────────┘       │ offset │          ├──────────────────────┤      ├──────────────────────┤
                 └────────┘          │ some_field           │      │ length (u64)         │
                                     │ vec.ptr  ●───────────┼─────►│ elem_0               │
  struct_borrow produces a fat       └──────────────────────┘      │ elem_1               │
  pointer into the struct field.                                   │ ...                  │
  VecPushBack writes the updated                                   └──────────────────────┘
  pointer directly into the struct.


Heap struct containing another heap struct
------------------------------------------

Frame slot           Heap (outer)                    Heap (inner)
┌────────┐    ┌──────────────────────┐        ┌──────────────────────┐
│   ●────┼───►│ desc_id(4) | size(4) │        │ desc_id(4) | size(4) │
└────────┘    ├──────────────────────┤        ├──────────────────────┤
              │ inner_ptr ●──────────┼───────►│ field_0              │
              │ other_field          │        │ field_1              │
              └──────────────────────┘        └──────────────────────┘


Reference to a primitive on the stack
-------------------------------------

Frame slot (the ref)             Stack frame
┌────────┐                       ┌────────┐  ← base
│ base ●─┼──────────────────────►│ ...    │
│ offset │                       │ ...    │
└────────┘                       │ ...    │
  16 bytes                       │ value  │  ← base + offset
                                 │ ...    │
                                 └────────┘


Reference to a heap struct on the stack
---------------------------------------

Frame slot (the ref)             Stack frame                      Heap
┌────────┐                       ┌────────┐  ← base       ┌──────────────────┐
│ base ●─┼──────────────────────►│ ...    │                │ desc_id | size   │
│ offset │                       │ ...    │                ├──────────────────┤
└────────┘                       │   ●────┼───────────────►│ field_0          │
  16 bytes                       │ ...    │  ← base+offset │ field_1          │
                                 │ ...    │                │ ...              │
                                 └────────┘                └──────────────────┘

  base points to the frame, offset points to the slot holding the owned pointer.
  *ref = new_struct: allocate new struct on heap, write new pointer at base+offset.
  The old struct becomes garbage (collected by GC).


Reference to a vector on the stack
-----------------------------------

Frame slot (vec_ref)             Stack frame                      Heap (data buffer)
┌────────┐                       ┌────────┐  ← base        ┌──────────────────┐
│ base ●─┼──────────────────────►│ ...    │                │ desc_id | size   │
│ offset │                       │ ...    │                ├──────────────────┤
└────────┘                       │   ●────┼───────────────►│ length           │
  16 bytes                       │ ...    │  ← base+offset │ elem_0           │
                                 │ ...    │  (vec_ptr slot) │ elem_1           │
                                 └────────┘                │ ...              │
                                                           └──────────────────┘

  base points to the frame, offset points to the 8-byte slot holding the
  vector heap pointer (or null if empty).
  vec.push_back through ref: may allocate/grow, writes new ptr at base+offset.
  Length and capacity live on the heap (capacity is derived from header size).


Reference Operations (pseudo code)
===================================

Borrow Local
------------
Given: a local variable at frame slot `slot_offset`
Produces: ref to that local

  fn borrow_local(frame_ptr, slot_offset) -> Ref:
      return (frame_ptr, slot_offset)

  No memory access -- just capture the frame pointer and slot offset.


Borrow Field Inline
-------------------
Given: ref = (base, offset) pointing to an inline struct
Produces: ref to a field within that inline struct

  fn borrow_field_inline(ref, field_offset) -> Ref:
      return (ref.base, ref.offset + field_offset)

  No memory access needed -- just adjust the offset.


Borrow Field Heap
-----------------
Given: ref = (base, offset) pointing to a slot holding an owned heap pointer
Produces: ref to a field within the heap struct

  fn borrow_field_heap(ref, field_offset) -> Ref:
      heap_ptr = *(ref.base + ref.offset)       // load the owned pointer
      return (heap_ptr, STRUCT_DATA_OFFSET + field_offset)

  One dereference to read the owned pointer. The resulting ref points
  directly into the heap struct's payload.
  - For primitive/inline fields: *ref = val overwrites bytes in place.
  - For owned pointer fields (heap struct, vector): *ref = val replaces
    the pointer/metadata in the heap struct's payload.


Borrow Vector Elem
------------------
Given: vec_ref = (base, offset) pointing to an 8-byte slot holding the
       vector's heap pointer
Produces: ref to the element at index idx

  fn borrow_vec_elem(vec_ref, idx, elem_size) -> Ref:
      data_ptr = *(vec_ref.base + vec_ref.offset)  // load the heap pointer
      return (data_ptr, VEC_DATA_OFFSET + idx * elem_size)

  One dereference to read the data buffer pointer. The resulting ref
  points into the data buffer's element region.
  - Safe as long as no growth occurs while the borrow is live
    (enforced by Move's borrow checker).


Notes
-----
- The data buffer has a universal header (desc_id + size) so the GC can
  determine object size when scanning the heap linearly (Cheney's algorithm).
- The GC traces element pointers inside the data buffer via the buffer's
  own descriptor (e.g. Trivial for buffers with no heap pointers in elements,
  or a Vector descriptor that knows the elem_ref_offsets).
- Growth reallocates the data buffer (new header + length + elements) and
  writes the new pointer back through the vec_ref fat pointer.
- Vector capacity is not stored explicitly — it is derived from the header's
  size_in_bytes field: cap = (size - VEC_DATA_OFFSET) / elem_size.
- A null vector pointer represents an empty vector with no heap allocation.
  VecNew writes null; the first VecPushBack allocates lazily.
- Heap struct/enum headers are 8 bytes: desc_id (u32) + size (u32).
